justification: All pass criteria met: Option B recommended with 5+ explicit reasons (retry via queue, channel extensibility, decoupling from provider failures, backpressure handling, clean separation of concerns). trade_offs lists both gains AND sacrifices for each decision. Event schema explicitly identified as rigidity_point ('DeliveryStatusChanged Event Schema' with versioning guidance). Channel extensibility identified as flexibility_point ('Adding push notifications or WhatsApp means implementing one new class'). implementation_guidance names concrete next steps including specific queue technology recommendations (SQS/Cloud Tasks/BullMQ with rationale), file paths, retry policy, DLQ design. Small-team constraint factored throughout (managed queue over Kafka/RabbitMQ, single worker over per-channel workers, explicit rejection of over-engineering). risks section includes queue infrastructure risk (dual-write problem, DLQ filling up unnoticed).

observations.text: Excellent calibration to team size — explicitly rejected Kafka, RabbitMQ, CQRS, and per-channel workers as premature for a 3-person team
Identified event schema as rigidity point with actionable mitigation (version from day one, use schema definition)
Implementation guidance is highly actionable: specific file paths, specific technology options with selection criteria, explicit pitfalls to avoid
DLQ alerting called out as a launch blocker, not a nice-to-have — demonstrates operational maturity in the recommendation
Idempotent consumer pattern included proactively, addressing a real-world concern with at-least-once semantics
Transactional outbox pattern mentioned as future evolution path without recommending it for day one — good YAGNI discipline
Output is verbose — the level of detail exceeds what a 3-person team needs for an architecture decision, though all content is relevant
trade_offs section covers three distinct decisions with gains, sacrifices, and alternatives considered for each — thorough and balanced

agent_output_excerpt: {"executive_summary":"For a small courier team (3 engineers) needing reliable, non-latency-critical delivery notifications across expandable channels, Option B (event-driven with a managed queue) is the recommended architecture — but scoped to a lightweight implementation using a managed service like AWS SQS or Google Cloud Tasks, not a full Kafka/RabbitMQ deployment. Option A (synchronous REST) is simpler to start but creates tight coupling, makes retry logic fragile, and turns channel expansio
